# Phase 1 — Exploratory Data Analysis (Stage A, synthetic regime)

**Regime:** S (synthetic). Every analysis is generated by `python -m bball.eval.run_eda
--config configs/eda.yaml` on synthetic sessions spanning the collection grid (two indoor
gyms, two outdoor courts, varied camera placement). Figures live in
`reports/figures/eda/`; the raw numbers in `reports/figures/eda/eda_findings.json`. Stage B
re-runs the **same commands** on real footage, and the sim-vs-real comparison doubles as the
synthetic-engine fidelity audit (plan gate G4).

EDA here is not decoration: each analysis de-risks a specific architecture decision (plan
§3). The table names the consumer of each.

| Analysis | Figure | Feeds |
|---|---|---|
| Ball apparent size vs placement | `eda_ball_size.png` | detector input resolution (A3); heatmap-vs-bbox framing |
| Motion blur vs frame rate | `eda_motion_blur.png` | fps recommendation; blur-augmentation realism |
| Occlusion vs camera azimuth | `eda_occlusion.png` | bridging design (A5); collection guidance |
| Rim ellipse roundness vs height | `eda_rim_geometry.png` | rim-normalized FSM viability; camera-placement guide |
| Trajectory statistics | `eda_trajectory_stats.png` | synthetic-engine grounding (G4); attempt thresholds |
| Class balance | `eda_class_balance.png` | split stratification; loss weighting |

---

## 1. Ball apparent size — we are in the 20–40 px regime, at its top edge

Median ball **diameter** is **34–38 px** across placements (indoor gyms ~34–36 px, outdoor
~38 px at the slightly larger framing distance), with the P10–P90 band spanning roughly
20–55 px over a full flight (the ball is largest near release/close to the camera, smallest
at the far arc).

**Consequence.** This sits at the **top of the 20–40 px band** the Phase-0 survey assumed
when it argued a basketball is *not* in the sub-10 px shuttlecock regime where per-frame
detection collapses. It supports the bbox-first decision (A1) and says input resolution
should be chosen so the *smallest* (far-corner) balls stay above ~15 px — i.e. 768 px is a
reasonable detector input, with 512 px risking the far-arc tail (A3 tests this).

## 2. Motion blur — 240 fps really is near-blur-free; 30 fps is not

Per-frame ball displacement (a proxy for streak length) by capture rate:

| Frame rate | Median streak (px) | P90 (px) |
|---|---|---|
| 240 fps | ~9 | ~19 |
| 60 fps | ~37 | ~75 |
| 30 fps | ~76 | ~150 |

**Consequence.** At 240 fps the ball moves ~9 px/frame — a crisp disc, confirming the
Phase-0 claim that slow-mo capture slashes per-frame blur. At 30 fps the ball smears ~76 px
between frames (multiple ball-diameters), which is exactly the regime where a single-frame
detector struggles and a temporal cue (or heavy directional-blur augmentation) earns its
keep. This grounds two decisions: (a) recommend 240 fps for rim-approach windows, and (b)
size the motion-blur augmentation kernels (Stage-B detector training) to the measured
60/30 fps streak lengths, not an arbitrary value.

## 3. Occlusion vs azimuth — the gaps the bridging layer must close, and where they live

Mean fraction of flight frames with the ball >50% occluded (behind rim/net/backboard):

| Camera azimuth | Occluded fraction |
|---|---|
| 30° | 0.26 |
| 45° | 0.24 |
| 60° | 0.13 |
| 90° (sideline) | 0.08 |

**Consequence.** Occlusion **falls sharply toward the sideline**: a 45° wing sees the ball
disappear into the rim/net region on ~a quarter of flight frames, a sideline camera on <10%.
This directly sizes the bridging problem (A5): the parabola must routinely span multi-frame
gaps at wing placements, and it is a collection-guidance input (elevate + move toward the
wing/side to shorten occlusions). The occlusion-gap *length* distribution — not just the
fraction — is what A5 sweeps (3–30 frames).

## 4. Rim ellipse roundness — DO NOT mount the camera at rim height (a real finding)

The rim is a horizontal circle; its image is an ellipse whose minor/major axis ratio (b/a)
measures how "round" — i.e. how observable — it is. Sweeping camera height at fixed distance:

| Camera height | b/a (roundness) |
|---|---|
| 1.5 m (tripod) | 0.17 |
| 2.5 m | 0.06 |
| **3.0 m (≈ rim height 3.048 m)** | **0.005 (edge-on)** |
| 3.5 m | 0.05 |
| 4.5 m (elevated) | 0.16 |

**Consequence — surfaced by the build, not anticipated in the plan.** A camera at ≈ rim
height views the rim **nearly edge-on** (b/a → 0), collapsing the rim-normalized minor axis
that the FSM and T5 depend on. Roundness recovers both *below* (1.5 m tripod) and *above*
(4.5 m) the rim plane. This has three consequences we adopted: (a) the FSM unit tests and the
A5/A8/A9 ablations use a **1.5 m tripod** placement where the rim ellipse is well-formed; (b)
the camera-placement guide now says *avoid ~2.7–3.3 m* (mount clearly below at tripod height,
or clearly above the rim); (c) it reinforces why short/long is the hard axis (A6) — the depth
information rides the minor axis that this geometry can erase. The A6 curve then shows the
azimuth half of the same story.

## 5. Trajectory statistics — the synthetic engine is grounded, not fantasy (gate G4)

Release angle and speed fall within the **cited** ranges (45–55°, 6–9 m/s) by construction,
with the close-shot tail extending to steeper angles (a layup/floater must clear the release-
to-rim height gap — a physical necessity the generator enforces). Median **apex height 3.82 m**
(clears the 3.048 m rim, as a real shot must) and median **flight time 0.97 s** are consistent
with amateur half-court shots. These distributions are the audit target for Stage B: if real
footage disagrees, the engine's parameter ranges get corrected and every synthetic number is
re-scoped.

## 6. Class balance — maker-biased, exactly as predicted

Across sessions: **136 makes / 104 misses (≈57% makes)** and a zone split of roughly
midrange 41% / 3PT 33% / short-range 26%.

**Consequence.** The ~57% make rate reproduces the §3 warning that self-collected data is
**maker-biased** (people practice what they make). Stage B corrects it via the shot-script's
deliberate-miss blocks; Stage-A evaluation stratifies by outcome so the make-heavy prior does
not flatter aggregate accuracy, and any learned head uses class weighting.

---

**Net:** the six analyses confirm two Phase-0 assumptions (20–40 px ball; 240 fps ≈
blur-free), quantify the occlusion the bridging layer must handle, ground the simulator in
cited physics, and surface one genuinely new constraint — **avoid mounting the camera at rim
height** — that shaped the FSM/ablation placements and the camera-placement guidance.
